items.q: At which electrode are positive ions reduced during electrolysis?
items.correct: At the cathode (the negative electrode) — positive ions gain electrons there.
items.misconception: Positive ions are reduced at the anode because that's where positive things go.
items.random_wrong: Positive ions are oxidised at the cathode by losing protons.
items.why: Students associate 'positive' with 'attracted to positive' or get muddled by the sign conventions and switch the electrodes.

items.q: What is reduction in terms of electrons?
items.correct: Gain of electrons (the oxidation number is reduced).
items.misconception: Reduction means losing oxygen and oxidation means gaining oxygen — full stop.
items.random_wrong: Reduction means a substance becomes physically smaller in size.
items.why: Students learn the oxygen definition first (from rusting/combustion) and never update to the more general electron-transfer definition.

items.q: What happens to electrons when sodium reacts with chlorine?
items.correct: Sodium loses one electron to chlorine; the resulting Na+ and Cl- ions attract electrostatically.
items.misconception: Sodium and chlorine share an electron between them.
items.random_wrong: Protons move from chlorine to sodium to balance the charges.
items.why: Students over-apply the covalent (sharing) model to all bonding because sharing is introduced first and feels intuitive.

items.q: Which metal will displace copper from copper sulfate solution?
items.why: It's Iron. “Silver” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: At which electrode do positive metal ions gain electrons?
items.why: Correct answer: Cathode (negative). “Anode (positive)” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In the standard reactivity series (K Na Ca Mg Al C Zn Fe H Cu Ag Au), what position is iron from the most reactive end? Count from 1.
items.why: The real figure is 8. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
